q: In an electrical circuit, a battery is connected to pass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of charge through it in a certain given time. The workdone by the battery is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>
question: In an electrical circuit, a battery is connected to pass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of charge through it in a certain given time. The potential difference between two plates of the battery is maintained at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The workdone by the battery is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>
solution:
Work done by battery =QΔV

⇒20×15=300 J

=300

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $8 \, \mu C$ and $-2 \, \mu C$ are located at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm, respectively on the $x$-axis. The ratio of electric flux due to these charges through two spheres of radii $3$ cm and $5$ cm with their centers at the origin is _______.
question: Two point charges $8 \, \mu C$ and $-2 \, \mu C$ are located at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm, respectively on the $x$-axis. The ratio of electric flux due to these charges through two spheres of radii $3$ cm and $5$ cm with their centers at the origin is _______.
solution:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}$.

For the first sphere of radius $r_1 = 3$ cm centered at the origin, only the charge at $x = 2$ cm is enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 1} = 8 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_1 = \dfrac{8 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

For the second sphere of radius $r_2 = 5$ cm centered at the origin, both charges at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm are enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 2} = 8 \, \mu\text{C} - 2 \, \mu\text{C} = 6 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_2 = \dfrac{6 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

The ratio of the electric flux is:

$\dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} = \dfrac{8}{6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4 : 3$

subject: Physics
q:
For a charged spherical ball, electrostatic potential inside the ball varies with r as V=2ar2+b.

Here, a and b are constant and r is the distance from the center. The volume charge density inside the ball is -λaε. The value of λ is ______.

ε= permittivity of medium.
question:
For a charged spherical ball, electrostatic potential inside the ball varies with r as V=2ar2+b.

Here, a and b are constant and r is the distance from the center. The volume charge density inside the ball is -λaε. The value of λ is ______.

ε= permittivity of medium.
solution:
As we know, E=-dVdr=-4ar

Now, electric field inside the charged sphere is given by E=ρr3ε. 

Comparing both, we get ρ=-12aε.

Therefore, λ=12.

subject: Physics
q: A two point charges 4q and –q are fixed on the x -axis at x=−d2 and x=d2, respectively. If the third point charge ‘q ’ is taken from the origin to x=d along the semicircle as shown in the figure, the energy of the charge will:
question: A two point charges 4q and –q are fixed on the x -axis at x=−d2 and x=d2, respectively. If the third point charge ‘q ’ is taken from the origin to x=d along the semicircle as shown in the figure, the energy of the charge will:
solution:
Potential at origin O, the potential due to two charges is given by

V0=4q4πε0d2+−q4πε0d2=6q4πε0d

Potential at P at a distance, x=d is given by

VP=4q4πε03d2+−q4πε0d2=q6πε0d

Change in potential energy due the charge motion of charge q is given by

 ∆U=qΔV=qVf−Vi

⇒∆U=qVP−VO

∆U=qq6πε0d−3q2πε0d=−4q23πε0d

q: Three concentric spherical metallic shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo><</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> have surface charge densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi>σ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, respectively. The shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> are at same potential. If the radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of shell <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: Three concentric spherical metallic shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo><</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> have surface charge densities <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>–</mo><mo> </mo><mi>σ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math>, respectively. The shells <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> are at same potential. If the radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Y</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>, respectively. The radius of shell <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
The charges on the spheres are

 qX=σ4πa2qY=-σ4πb2qZ=σ4πc2 

It is given that VX=VZ

So, 

qX4πε0a+qY4πε0b+qZ4πε0c=qX4πε0c+qY4πε0c+qZ4πε0c⇒σ4πa2a-σ4πb2b+σ4πc2c=σ4π(a2-b2+c2)c⇒c(a-b+c)=(a2-b2+c2)⇒c=a+b=2 cm+3 cm=5 cm

subject: Physics
q:
Ques:
$\sigma$ is the uniform surface charge density of a thin spherical shell of radius $R$. The electric field at any point on the surface of the spherical shell is :
question:
Ques:
$\sigma$ is the uniform surface charge density of a thin spherical shell of radius $R$. The electric field at any point on the surface of the spherical shell is :
solution: By Gauss law $\int \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{in}}}{\varepsilon_0}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{EdA}=\frac{\sigma \times \mathrm{dA}}{\varepsilon_0} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is to be divided into two. The distance between the two divided charges is constant. The magnitude of the divided charges so that the force between them is maximum, will be:
question: A charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is to be divided into two. The distance between the two divided charges is constant. The magnitude of the divided charges so that the force between them is maximum, will be:
solution:
If after division, one of the charge is q the other charge will be 4-q. The force between them can be written as,

F=Kq4-qd2

For the force between them to be maximum,

dFdq=0⇒Kd24-2q=0

⇒q=2 μC

subject: Physics
q: Five positive charges each having charge $q$ are placed at the vertices of a pentagon as shown in the figure. The electric potential $(V)$ and the electric field $(\vec{E})$ at the center $O$ of the pentagon due to these five positive charges are:
question: Five positive charges each having charge $q$ are placed at the vertices of a pentagon as shown in the figure. The electric potential $(V)$ and the electric field $(\vec{E})$ at the center $O$ of the pentagon due to these five positive charges are:
solution:
The electric potential at center O is the scalar sum of contributions from all 5 charges.
Each charge is equidistant from center, so $V = 5 cdot \frac{kq}{r} = \frac{5q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}$.
The electric field is a vector sum of field vectors from all 5 charges.
Due to 5-fold rotational symmetry of a regular pentagon, the 5 field vectors point radially outward from center, equally spaced at 72° angles.
The vector sum of 5 equally spaced unit vectors is zero, so $\vec{E} = 0$.

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
question: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos \theta \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos 180^{\circ}+\mathrm{PE} \cos 0^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=2 \mathrm{PE} \\ & =2 \times\left(4 \times 10^{-6}\right)(18) \times 10^4 \\ & =144 \times 10^{-2} \\ & =14.4 \mathrm{~mJ}\end{aligned}$

solution:
Given: E→netP=0

⇒kqx2=k×3qr-x2

⇒r-x2=3x2

⇒r-x=3x

⇒x=r3+1

When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. question: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The string make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. If density of material of the sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the dielectric constant of the liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
For first case:

Tcosθ=mg and 

Tsinθ=Fe

Therefore, tanθ=Femg

tanθ=FeρBVg....(i)

For second case:

tanθ=FekρBVg-ρLVg.....(ii) 

From Eq. (i) & (ii), we get

ρBVg=ρB-ρLkVg

⇒1.4 = 0.7 k 

⇒k = 2

q: Let $P(r)=\frac{Q}{\pi R^4} r$ be the charge density distribution for a solid sphere of radius $R$ and total charge $Q$. for a point ' $p$ ' inside the sphere at distance $r_1$ from the centre of the sphere, the magnitude of electric field is
question: Let $P(r)=\frac{Q}{\pi R^4} r$ be the charge density distribution for a solid sphere of radius $R$ and total charge $Q$. for a point ' $p$ ' inside the sphere at distance $r_1$ from the centre of the sphere, the magnitude of electric field is
solution: $$ E 4 \pi r_1^2=\frac{\int_0^{r_1} \frac{Q}{\pi R^4} r 4 \pi r^2 d r}{\varepsilon_0} \Rightarrow E=\frac{Q r_1^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 R^4} . $$

subject: Physics
q: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
question: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
solution:
The potential is constant inside the sphere as the electric field is zero inside the sphere. So, the value is given by 

V=Q4πε0R for r<R

The potential at the surface is 

V=Q4πε0R

The potential outside the sphere starts decreasing and is given by the formula 

V=Q4πε0r for r>R

q: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
question: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
solution:
An electric dipole is a system of two charges of equal and opposite magnitude placed at a short distance.

The dipole moment is, p=2ql.

The electric field due to the dipole is,

E=2kpr3

Clearly, the electric field varies as 1r3.

q: A line charge of length $\frac{\text { ' } a \text { ' }}{2}$ is kept at the center of an edge $B C$ of a cube $A B C D E F G H$ having edge length ' $a$ ' as shown in the figure. If the density of line charge is $\lambda$ C per unit length, then the total electric flux through all the faces of the cube will be _______. (Take, $\epsilon_0$ as the free space permittivity)
question: A line charge of length $\frac{\text { ' } a \text { ' }}{2}$ is kept at the center of an edge $B C$ of a cube $A B C D E F G H$ having edge length ' $a$ ' as shown in the figure. If the density of line charge is $\lambda$ C per unit length, then the total electric flux through all the faces of the cube will be _______. (Take, $\epsilon_0$ as the free space permittivity)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Charge of the line charge }=\frac{a \lambda}{2} \\ & \text { Portion of wire inside cube }=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \therefore \quad q_{e n}=\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{a \lambda}{2}\right)=\frac{a \lambda}{8} \\ & \qquad \phi=\frac{q_{e n}}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{a \lambda}{8 \varepsilon_0}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Electrostatic field lines form closed loops.
B. The electric field lines point radially outward when charge is greater than zero.
C. The Gauss - Law is valid only for inverse - square force.
D. The workdone in moving a charged particle in a static electric field around a closed path is zero.
E. The motion of a particle under Coulomb's force must take place in a plane.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Electrostatic field lines form closed loops.
B. The electric field lines point radially outward when charge is greater than zero.
C. The Gauss - Law is valid only for inverse - square force.
D. The workdone in moving a charged particle in a static electric field around a closed path is zero.
E. The motion of a particle under Coulomb's force must take place in a plane.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement A: Electrostatic field lines originate from positive charges and terminate on negative charges. They do not form closed loops because the electrostatic field is conservative. Thus, A is incorrect.
Statement B: For a positive point charge ($q > 0$), the electric field lines are directed radially outwards. Thus, B is correct.
Statement C: Gauss's Law is a consequence of the inverse-square nature of Coulomb's law ($F \propto 1/r^2$). If the force law were different, Gauss's Law would not hold in its standard form. Thus, C is correct.
Statement D: The electrostatic field is conservative, meaning the work done in moving a charge along any closed path is zero ($\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$). Thus, D is correct.
Statement E: Coulomb's force is a central force. The motion of a particle under a central force always takes place in a plane because the torque about the center is zero, keeping angular momentum constant in direction. Thus, E is correct.
Since B, C, D, and E are correct, the correct option is (4).

subject: Physics
q:
Experimentally it is found that 12.8 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. So the orbital radius of the electron in a hydrogen atom is 9x×10-10 m.

The value of the x is: _____.

(1 eV=1.6×10-19 J, 14πε0=9×109 Nm2C2 and electronic charge =1.6×10-19 C)
question:
Experimentally it is found that 12.8 eV energy is required to separate a hydrogen atom into a proton and an electron. So the orbital radius of the electron in a hydrogen atom is 9x×10-10 m.

The value of the x is: _____.

(1 eV=1.6×10-19 J, 14πε0=9×109 Nm2C2 and electronic charge =1.6×10-19 C)
solution:
The given data is 

q=1.6×10-19 C14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2

The formula for the value of energy in a hydrogen atom is given by 

E=q28πε0r⇒E=q22(4πε0)r

⇒r=9×109×1.6×10-1912.8×2

⇒r=916×10-10 m

It is given that r=9x×10-10 m⇒916×10-10=9x×10-10 ⇒x=16

subject: Physics
q: For a uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the electric field on its axis has the largest magnitude at a distance <math><mi>h</mi></math> from its centre. Then value of <math><mi>h</mi></math> is:
question: For a uniformly charged ring of radius <math><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the electric field on its axis has the largest magnitude at a distance <math><mi>h</mi></math> from its centre. Then value of <math><mi>h</mi></math> is:
solution:
Electric field at a distance h from centre on axis

E=14πε0QhR2+h232

To get maximum E, dEdh=0

0=14πε0QR2+h232-Qh32R2+h2122hR2+h23

⇒h=±R2

q: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9 q is placed at $(\mathrm{d}, 0,0)$ in Cartesian coordinate system. The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is :
question: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9 q is placed at $(\mathrm{d}, 0,0)$ in Cartesian coordinate system. The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is :
solution:
Let $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{p}}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{x}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{k} 9 \mathrm{q}}{(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{x})^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ co-ordinate of P is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}, 0,0\right)$

subject: Physics
q:
Two long currents carrying thin wires, both with current I, are held by insulating threads of length L and are in equilibrium as shown in the figure, with threads making an angle ' θ ' with the vertical. If wires have a mass λ per unit length then the value of I is:
(g= gravitational acceleration)
question:
Two long currents carrying thin wires, both with current I, are held by insulating threads of length L and are in equilibrium as shown in the figure, with threads making an angle ' θ ' with the vertical. If wires have a mass λ per unit length then the value of I is:
(g= gravitational acceleration)
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>θ</mi><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi><mi>g</mi><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math>
solution:
Two wires will repel each other due to magnetic force, then the magnetic force per unit length is, dfdl=μ0I22π2Lsinθ=μ0I24πLsinθ. And mass per unit length of each wire=dmdl=λ. So, the magnetic force on the total length L of the wire is fm=μ0I2L4πLsinθ, and weight =λLg. By equilibrium of wire,  Tsinθ=fm  &  Tcosθ = W = λl'g⇒TsinθTcosθ=fmmg⇒fm=λl'gtanθ
⇒μ0I24πLsinθL=λLgsinθcosθ

⇒I2=λgπLμ0cosθ4sin2θ⇒I=2sinθλπgLμ0cosθ

q:
As shown in the figure, a configuration of two equal point charges q0=+2μC is placed on an inclined plane. Mass of each point charge is 20 g. Assume that there is no friction between charge and plane. For the system of two point charges to be in equilibrium (at rest) the height h=x×10-3 m. The value of x is
 Take 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2, g=10 m s-2
question:
As shown in the figure, a configuration of two equal point charges q0=+2μC is placed on an inclined plane. Mass of each point charge is 20 g. Assume that there is no friction between charge and plane. For the system of two point charges to be in equilibrium (at rest) the height h=x×10-3 m. The value of x is
 Take 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2, g=10 m s-2
solution:
For the condition of equilibrium, the Coulomb force is equal to mgsinθ. Hence,

kq024h2=(mgsinθ)⇒kq024h2=20×10-3×10×12

⇒9×109×4×10−124h2=20×10-3×10×12⇒h=9100 m=0.3 m=300×10-3 m

h2=9100⇒h=310m=0.3m

=300×10−3m

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' and charge ' $q$ ' is fastened to one end ' $A$ ' of a massless string having equilibrium length $l$, whose other end is fixed at point ' $O$ '. The whole system is placed on a frictionless horizontal plane and is initially at rest. If uniform electric field is switched on along the direction as shown in figure, then the speed of the particle when it crosses the x -axis is
question: A particle of mass ' $m$ ' and charge ' $q$ ' is fastened to one end ' $A$ ' of a massless string having equilibrium length $l$, whose other end is fixed at point ' $O$ '. The whole system is placed on a frictionless horizontal plane and is initially at rest. If uniform electric field is switched on along the direction as shown in figure, then the speed of the particle when it crosses the x -axis is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{all}}=\Delta \mathrm{k} \\ & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \mathrm{qE} \frac{\ell}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2-0 \\ & \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{qE} \ell}{\mathrm{m}}}\end{aligned}$

solution:
V=cμrεr
=3.33×107 m sec-1

subject: Physics
q: The potential at a point $x$ (measured in $\mu \mathrm{m}$ ) due to some charges situated on the $\mathrm{x}$-axis is given by $\mathrm{V(x)=20 /\left(x^2-4\right)} \mathrm{~Volts}$. The electric field $\mathrm{E}$ at $\mathrm{x=4 ~\mu m}$ is given by
question: The potential at a point $x$ (measured in $\mu \mathrm{m}$ ) due to some charges situated on the $\mathrm{x}$-axis is given by $\mathrm{V(x)=20 /\left(x^2-4\right)} \mathrm{~Volts}$. The electric field $\mathrm{E}$ at $\mathrm{x=4 ~\mu m}$ is given by
solution:
$\mathrm{V_x=\frac{20}{x^2-4}}$
$\mathrm{E=-\frac{d V}{d x}=\frac{20}{\left(x^2-4\right)^2}(2 x-0)=\frac{160}{144}=\frac{10}{9}}$
